nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically encountered in sheltered bays and lagoons at depths of roughly 1-12 m, where it forms dense aggregations hovering above mounds of branching coral. Tends to shelter among branching corals (commonly Acropora) and emerges to forage at dusk.

diet.notes: Carnivorous planktivore. In the wild it picks small zooplankton and crustaceans from the water column above branching coral heads. In captivity it generally accepts a varied diet of mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, bloodworms, and quality marine pellets or flakes once settled, though small bite-sized meaty foods tend to work best given the small mouth. Evening or dusk feeding tends to improve acceptance given the species' nocturnal foraging tendency.

avoidWith: fast boisterous tankmates that outcompete at feeding

summary: A small (~6-7 cm) translucent Indo-Pacific cardinalfish with iridescent blue eye markings and a yellow caudal spot. Generally hardy and beginner-friendly, peaceful and reef-safe, but strongly shoaling — best kept in groups of 4 or more.
careNotes:
The Longspine Cardinalfish is a small, mostly translucent apogonid widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific from the Red Sea and Mozambique east to Samoa and Tonga, north to the Ryukyu Islands and south to New Caledonia. In the wild it forms dense aggregations hovering above mounds of branching coral in sheltered bays and lagoons, generally at depths of 1-12 m. The body is largely transparent with scattered iridescent blue and yellow spots, and the large black eyes are outlined in iridescent blue — a distinctive 'blue-eye' look that is the basis for several trade names.

It is generally considered a beginner-friendly, peaceful, reef-safe species that does not bother corals or most invertebrates. The most important husbandry caveat is grouping: the species is strongly shoaling and tends to be reclusive, reluctant to feed, and prone to poor long-term outcomes when kept singly or in pairs. Aquarium-hobby sources consistently advise groups of at least 4 individuals, with 10 or more being ideal. In a group the fish hovers in mid-water above rockwork and feeds readily; on its own it often hides and refuses food.

The species is largely nocturnal and tends to shelter among branching corals or in shaded rockwork during the day, emerging at dusk to pick zooplankton. Plenty of branching-coral or live-rock shelters and shaded retreats are generally advised, alongside an unobstructed mid-water swimming zone above the rockwork. Evening feeding tends to improve acceptance for newly added specimens.

Reef-safety has one minor caveat. Although the species itself will not damage corals or sessile invertebrates, larger specimens have been reported to take very small ornamental shrimp, tiny juvenile gobies, or other bite-sized tankmates on occasion. Most sources still classify it as reef-compatible.

Tank-size note: LiveAquaria lists a 10 US gal (~38 L) minimum and aquarium-hobby profiles cite 20 US gal (~76 L), but those figures assume a single specimen or pair — which, per the same sources, is not how the species should be kept. A small shoal of 4-10 individuals plus appropriate rockwork is more honestly accommodated by ~100 L with at least a 75 cm footprint, so the recommendation written here biases upward from the retailer minimum.

Water parameters should sit in the typical tropical-marine reef range: temperature roughly 24-27°C (hobby sources cite 22-26°C / 72-78°F; FishBase wild-preferred is 26.6-29.3°C, so 24-27°C is the cross-source average), pH 8.1-8.4, specific gravity 1.022-1.025, and alkalinity around 8-12 dKH. Lifespan was not stated by the sources reviewed.

Note on monogamy: FishBase characterizes the species as monogamous with home-ranging pair behavior in the wild. This appears to be a wild-population observation; aquarium-hobby practice strongly favors keeping shoals of 4+ rather than isolated pairs, because captive individuals housed solo or as a pair are reported to do poorly. Both observations can be true simultaneously — paired courtship within a larger shoal is consistent with apogonid biology.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder, like other apogonids. The male carries the egg mass in his buccal cavity for roughly 1-3 weeks and largely ceases feeding during brooding (a visibly swollen jaw is the most obvious sign). Spawning has been observed in captivity in well-fed shoals, though dedicated breeding setups generally improve fry survival as larvae are pelagic and vulnerable to predation in community tanks.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source — gave scientific name (Zoramia leptacantha), family (Apogonidae), care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), minimum tank size (10 gal — flagged as understated; see careNotes tank-size rationale), reef-compatible (yes), diet (meaty carnivore), max size 2.5 in, water parameters 72-78°F / pH 8.1-8.4 / SG 1.022-1.025 / dKH 8-12, Indian Ocean reefs origin, slow methodical swimmer that hides in seagrass or among long-spined urchins.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority and wild-biology source: Apogonidae, Kurtiformes; max size 6.0 cm TL; depth 1-12 m; Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea and Mozambique east to Samoa and Tonga, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to New Caledonia, throughout Micronesia); preferred wild temperature 26.6-29.3°C (mean 28.6); forms dense aggregations above branching coral in sheltered bays and lagoons; nocturnal; characterised as monogamous with home-ranging pair behaviour in the wild. Page uses the spelling 'Zoramia leptacanthus'; both endings are used in the literature for this species, with leptacantha being the more common LiveAquaria/aquarium-trade form.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: 20 gal min, 72-80°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.022-1.025, dKH 8-12, ~3 in max, peaceful, reef compatible. Critically emphasizes the species must be kept in groups of at least 4 (10+ ideal) — solo or paired individuals become 'reclusive, reluctant feeders' and do poorly. Notes larger individuals may consume very small gobies or shrimp on occasion. Drove the upward bias on minVolumeLiters and the minGroupSize=4 decision.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki
additional.notes: Confirmed ~2.4 in (6 cm) adult size and 20 gal recommended minimum; reinforces the group-of-4+ shoaling requirement. Cross-checked the peaceful temperament and reef-safe classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder with apogonid pair courtship; male carries egg mass in buccal cavity for roughly 1-3 weeks and stops feeding during brooding. Spawning in captivity has been observed in well-fed shoals, but routine fry rearing is uncommon and generally requires a dedicated setup.
